verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It would be possible for me also to paint you in your own colours, and to meet your insanity with a similar rage; to say what I know and add what I do not know; and with a license like yours, or rather fury and madness, to keep up things false and true alike, till I was ashamed to speak and you to hear: and to upbraid you in such a way as would condemn either the accused or the accuser; to force myself on the reader by mere effrontery, make him believe that what I wrote unscrupulously I wrote truly.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But far be it from the practice of Christians while offering up their lives to seek the life of others, and to become homicides not with the sword but the will.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This may agree with your gentleness and innocence; for you can draw forth from the dung heap within your breast alike the odour of roses and the stench of corpses; and, contrary to the precept of the Prophet, call that bitter which once you had praised as sweet.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But it is not necessary for us, in treating of Christian topics, to throw out accusations which ought to be brought before the law courts.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You shall hear nothing more from me than the vulgar saying: “When you have said what you like, you shall bear what you do not like.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Or if the coarse proverb seems to you too vulgar, and, being a man of culture, you prefer the words of philosophers or poets, take from me the words of Homer.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “What words thou speakest, thou the like shalt hear.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: One thing I should like to learn from one of such eminent sanctity and fastidiousness, (whose holiness is such that in the presence of your very handkerchiefs and aprons the devils cry out); whom do you take for your model in your writings?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Has any one of the catholic writers, in a controversy of opinions, imputed moral offences to the man with whom he is arguing?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Have your masters taught you to do this?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is this the system in which you have been trained, that, when you cannot answer a man, you should take off his head? that when you cannot silence a man’s tongue, you should cut it out?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You have nothing much to boast of, for you are doing only what the scorpions and cantharides do.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This is what Fulvia did to Cicero and Herodias to John.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: They could not bear to hear the truth, and therefore they pierced the tongue that spoke truth with the pin that parted their hair.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The duty of dogs is to bark in their masters’ service; why may I not bark in the service of Christ?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Many have written against Marcion or Valentinus, Arius or Eunomius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: By which of them was any accusation brought of immoral conduct?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Did they not in each case bring their whole effort to bear upon the refutation of the heresy?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is the machination of the heretics, that is of your masters, when convicted of betrayal of the faith, to betake themselves to evil speaking.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: So Eustathius the Bishop of Antioch was made into a father unawares.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: So Athanasius Bishop of Alexandria cut off a third hand of Arsenius; for, when he appeared alive after having been supposed to be dead, he was found to have two.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Such things also now are falsely charged against the Bishop of the same church, and the true faith is assailed by gold, which constitutes the power of yourself and your friends.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But I need not speak of controversy with heretics, who, though they are really without, yet call themselves Christians.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How many of our writers have contended with those most impious men, Celsus and Porphyry! but which of them has left the cause he was engaged in to busy himself with the imputation of crime to his adversary, such as ought to be set down not in church-writings but in the calendar of the judge?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For what advantage have you gained if you establish a man’s criminality but fail in your argument?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is quite unnecessary that in bringing an accusation you should risk your own head.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If your object is revenge, you can hire an executioner, and satisfy your desire.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You pretend to dread a scandal, and yet you are ready to kill a man who was once your brother, whom you now accuse, and whom you always treat as an enemy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Yet I wonder how a man like you, who knows what he is about, should be so blinded by madness as to wish to confer a benefit upon me by drawing forth my soul out of prison, and should not suffer it to remain with you in the darkness of this world.
